timeline.event: Born on October 15 in Röcken, Saxony, Prussia, as the son of a Lutheran pastor.
His younger sister Elisabeth Nietzsche was born.
His younger brother Ludwig Joseph was born.
His father, Carl Wilhelm Ludwig Nietzsche, passed away from a brain disease.
His younger brother died.
Attended a boys' school.
Started attending the Domgymnasium in Naumburg.
When his grandmother passed away, the family moved into her house.
Enrolled in Schulpforta and continued his studies until 1864.
After graduating from Schulpforta, he began studying theology and ancient philosophy at the University of Bonn.
Became acquainted with Schopenhauer's writings, and in October, moved to the University of Leipzig following Ritschl.
Read Friedrich Albert Lange's book 'History of Materialism and Critique of its Present Importance'.
Volunteered for the army and enlisted in October, and wrote 'The History of the Theognidea'.
Suffered a horseback riding accident in March, making him unable to continue military service, and began meeting the musician Richard Wagner.

Appointed professor of classical philology at the University of Basel in Switzerland, and received his doctorate from the University of Leipzig without an examination or dissertation. Wrote 'Homer and Classical Philology'.

His health deteriorated further, and he wrote 'Analecta Laertiana', 'Der florentinische Tractat über Homer und Hesiod', 'The Greek Music Drama', 'Philosophy in the Tragic Age of the Greeks', and 'Socrates and Tragedy'.
Wrote 'The Birth of Tragedy'.
Began collecting posthumous records such as philosophy appearing in ancient Greek tragedies.
Wrote 'Untimely Meditations'.
Formed a friendship with Paul Rée, and distanced himself from Wagner after being disappointed at the Bayreuth Festival.
Published 'Human, All Too Human'.
Resigned from his professorship at the University of Basel due to deteriorating health.
Planned to travel to Tunis but gave it up, and was inspired by the idea of eternal recurrence in Sils Maria. Wrote 'The Dawn'.
Wrote 'The Gay Science'.
Wrote 'Thus Spoke Zarathustra'.
Wrote 'Beyond Good and Evil'.
Wrote 'On the Genealogy of Morality'.
Wrote 'The Case of Wagner', 'Twilight of the Idols', 'The Antichrist', 'Ecce Homo', 'Dionysian-Dithyrambs', and 'Nietzsche contra Wagner'.
Collapsed in Turin, Italy in January and was admitted to a psychiatric hospital, losing his sanity.
Died in Weimar on August 25.
'The Will to Power', an edited version of Nietzsche's posthumous works, was published.
The copyright protection period for Nietzsche's works expired.

keyAchievements.title: Philosopher and Writer
keyAchievements.description: As a German philosopher and writer, he sought to break Western traditions and establish new values.

keyAchievements.title: Earned the Nickname 'Philosopher with a Hammer'
keyAchievements.description: Because he sought to break Western traditions and establish new values, he earned the nickname 'philosopher with a hammer'.

keyAchievements.title: Research on the Origins of Christian Morality and Rationalism
keyAchievements.description: Devoted himself to uncovering the origins of Christian morality and rationalism, arguing that rational things originated from irrationality and madness.

keyAchievements.title: Advocated 'Loving Life on Earth'
keyAchievements.description: Opposed idealistic dichotomy and advocated for loving life in reality.

keyAchievements.title: Critique of Totalitarianism, Chauvinism, and Anti-Semitism
keyAchievements.description: Spread ideas criticizing totalitarianism, chauvinism, nationalism, and anti-Semitism.

keyAchievements.title: Appointed Professor of Classical Philology at the University of Basel
keyAchievements.description: At age 24, he was appointed professor of classical philology at the University of Basel in Switzerland.

keyAchievements.title: Obtained Doctorate
keyAchievements.description: Received a doctorate from the University of Leipzig in 1869 based solely on his published writings.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote 'The Birth of Tragedy'
keyAchievements.description: In 1872, he wrote 'The Birth of Tragedy', containing an original aesthetic theory about ancient Greek tragedy.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote 'Untimely Meditations'
keyAchievements.description: Between 1873 and 1876, he wrote 'Untimely Meditations', containing cultural criticism that challenged the development of German culture.

keyAchievements.title: Published 'Human, All Too Human'
keyAchievements.description: In 1878, he published 'Human, All Too Human', full of his characteristic aphorisms, beginning his departure from the philosophies of Schopenhauer and Wagner.

keyAchievements.title: Presented the Concept of 'Death of God'
keyAchievements.description: Presented the concept of the 'Death of God', meaning the loss of the highest values created by humans and the advent of nihilism.

keyAchievements.title: Advocated Monism (Vital Ontology)
keyAchievements.description: Advocated a monism contrary to the dualism of modern philosophy centered on the will to power, breaking away from the distinction between subject and object.

keyAchievements.title: Distinguished Concepts of Nihilism
keyAchievements.description: Divided nihilism into active and passive nihilism, emphasizing the importance of an attitude that creates meaning for oneself.

keyAchievements.title: Presented the Idea of 'Eternal Recurrence'
keyAchievements.description: Presented the idea of 'eternal recurrence', inspired in Sils Maria in 1881, as the fundamental thought of 'Thus Spoke Zarathustra'.

keyAchievements.title: Presented the Concept of 'Übermensch'
keyAchievements.description: Conceived the 'Übermensch', a human type who overcomes and creates by moving beyond modern values, morality, and faith.

keyAchievements.title: Presented the Concept of 'Will to Power'
keyAchievements.description: As a concept opposing the instinct of self-preservation, he presented the 'will to power', arguing that the purpose of life is not survival but the exertion of power.

keyAchievements.title: Moral Criticism
keyAchievements.description: Classified morality into 'slave morality' and 'master morality', and criticized morality by examining its genealogy from psychological and axiological perspectives.

keyAchievements.title: Contributions as a Classical Philologist
keyAchievements.description: Influenced modern aesthetic theory based on a deep understanding of ancient Greece and Rome.

keyAchievements.title: Profound Posthumous Influence on European Philosophy and Literature
keyAchievements.description: Though ignored during his lifetime, he influenced numerous thinkers and writers posthumously, including Heidegger, Jaspers, Freud, and Thomas Mann.

keyAchievements.title: Composing Activities
keyAchievements.description: Composed lieder and piano pieces from about age 13 to 20, and left several compositions during his time in Basel.

faq.question: Why was Nietzsche called the 'philosopher with a hammer'?
faq.answer: He earned the nickname 'philosopher with a hammer' because he sought to shatter Western traditions and establish new values.

faq.question: What was the cause of Nietzsche's mental illness?
faq.answer: He suffered a mental breakdown in his later years; rumors that it was due to syphilis are just hearsay, and according to a Telegraph article, it was due to a malignant brain tumor.

faq.question: Was Nietzsche anti-Semitic?
faq.answer: Nietzsche criticized totalitarianism, chauvinism, nationalism, and anti-Semitism, but his book 'The Antichrist' explicitly contains content that provides a basis for hatred of Jews.

faq.question: Is Nietzsche's philosophy related to Nazism?
faq.answer: Nietzsche criticized totalitarianism, nationalism, and anti-Semitism, but after his death, his sister Elisabeth distorted his works to favor the Nazi regime. However, there are also points made that his officially published works were also utilized by the Nazis.

faq.question: What kind of evaluation did Nietzsche receive during his lifetime?
faq.answer: During his lifetime, he was thoroughly ignored by academia, harshly criticized by religious circles and moralists, and suffered from slander that he was corrupting society.

timeline.event: Born in the Prussian commercial city of Königsberg.
Entered the Collegium Fridericianum, a private school directed by theologian Schultz, who was an acquaintance of his mother.
Graduated from the Collegium Fridericianum and enrolled at the University of Königsberg to study philosophy and mathematics.
His father passed away, and he published his graduation thesis 'Thoughts on the True Estimation of Living Forces'.

Received his doctorate with the dissertation 'Universal Natural History and Theory of the Heavens' and gained the qualification to lecture at the university with the habilitation thesis 'A New Elucidation of the First Principles of Metaphysical Cognition'.
Declined a literature professorship offered by the Prussian Ministry of Education.
Got a job as a librarian at the Royal Library to cover living expenses.
Obtained a professorship in philosophy at the University of Königsberg and published his inaugural dissertation.
Finished his work as a librarian at the Royal Library.
Published the first edition of the 'Critique of Pure Reason'.
Published the essays 'Answering the Question: What Is Enlightenment?' and 'Idea for a Universal History with a Cosmopolitan Aim'.
Published 'Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals'.
Published 'Metaphysical Foundations of Natural Science' and was elected rector of the University of Königsberg until 1788.
Published the second edition of the 'Critique of Pure Reason'.
Published the 'Critique of Practical Reason'.
Published the 'Critique of Judgment'.
Had a conflict with the faculty administration over the publication and censorship of the essay 'On the Radical Evil in Human Nature'.
Published 'Religion within the Boundaries of Mere Reason'.
Published 'Perpetual Peace: A Philosophical Sketch'.
Published 'The Metaphysics of Morals'.
Passed away at 4:00 AM on February 12 at the age of 80.

keyAchievements.title: Established the Foundation of German Idealism
keyAchievements.description: Brought modern Enlightenment to its peak and established the foundation of German idealism.

keyAchievements.title: Established Critical Philosophy
keyAchievements.description: Established critical philosophy to overcome conventional empiricism and dogmatism.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote 'Critique of Pure Reason'
keyAchievements.description: Researched the structure and limits inherent in reason itself, attacking traditional metaphysics and epistemology while highlighting his own contributions.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote 'Critique of Practical Reason'
keyAchievements.description: Focused on ethics and found the basis of morality in transcendental freedom not governed by causality.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote 'Critique of Judgment'
keyAchievements.description: Showcased the essence of critical philosophy by studying aesthetics, teleology, and more.

keyAchievements.title: Attempted a Copernican Revolution
keyAchievements.description: Pointed out the limits of human reason and attempted a 'Copernican Revolution' by introducing transcendental forms into human cognition.

keyAchievements.title: Presented the Categorical Imperative
keyAchievements.description: Valued the motive more than the result of an action, presenting the categorical imperative as a command of duty that must be unconditionally followed in any situation.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed Perpetual Peace
keyAchievements.description: Proposed a League of Nations based on the concept of international law to realize a perpetual peace without wars between countries.

faq.question: What are Kant's major works?
faq.answer: Kant's major works include the 'Critique of Pure Reason', 'Critique of Practical Reason', and 'Critique of Judgment'.

faq.question: What does the 'Copernican Revolution' mean in Kant's epistemology?
faq.answer: The 'Copernican Revolution' is the idea that humans do not perceive objects exactly as they are, but rather human cognition creates the ideas of the objects.

faq.question: What are the two commands of the moral law presented by Kant?
faq.answer: The first is to act only according to that maxim whereby you can at the same time will that it should become a universal law, and the second is to act in such a way that you treat humanity, whether in your own person or in the person of any other, never merely as a means to an end, but always at the same time as an end.

faq.question: What is unique about Kant's life?
faq.answer: Kant never traveled more than 100 miles away from Königsberg in his entire life and lived a highly regular daily routine.

timeline.event: Born on March 31 to a noble family of magistrates in La Haye en Touraine.
Entered the Jesuit-run Collège Royal Henry-Le-Grand at La Flèche and received education until 1614.
Obtained a Licence in law from the University of Poitiers.
Heard a difficult geometry problem from a Dutch university principal in Paris and solved it.
Traveled to the Netherlands and met Isaac Beeckman, and wrote his first short work, 'Compendium Musicæ'.
Enlisted in the army of Maximilian I of Bavaria, experienced the Battle of White Mountain, and had a dream in a barrack near Ulm that illuminated his life's path.
Discharged from the army and returned to France.
Began researching mathematics and natural sciences, especially optics, in Paris.
Wrote the unfinished treatise 'Rules for the Direction of the Mind'.
Wrote the fragment 'Rules for the Direction of the Mind', and moved to the Netherlands that winter seeking freedom for research and contemplation.
Wrote 'The World' (Le Monde), a book on natural science.
Published 'Discourse on the Method', anonymously appending three parts: Dioptrics, Meteorology, and Geometry.
Published 'Meditations on First Philosophy'.
Systematically organized his philosophy and published 'Principles of Philosophy' in Latin.

Letters sent to Elisabeth of the Palatinate were collected and published as his last book 'The Passions of the Soul', and he went to the Swedish royal palace at the invitation of Queen Christina of Sweden.
Caught pneumonia and passed away at the age of 54 in Stockholm on February 11.
His remains returned to Paris and were placed in the Abbey of Saint Genevieve-du-Mont.
The French government moved his remains to the Panthéon.
His remains were moved again and placed in the Abbey of Saint-Germain-des-Prés.

keyAchievements.title: Established the Foundation of Modern Philosophy
keyAchievements.description: Is referred to as the founder of modern philosophy by first establishing the basic framework of modern thought.

keyAchievements.title: Founded Analytic Geometry
keyAchievements.description: Devised the Cartesian coordinate system and founded analytic geometry, systematically integrating algebra and geometry.

keyAchievements.title: Established 'I think, therefore I am'
keyAchievements.description: In his major work 'Discourse on the Method', he first established the fundamental principle of Enlightenment thought regarding the 'autonomous and rational subject' with 'I think, therefore I am'.

keyAchievements.title: Advocated Mind-Body Dualism
keyAchievements.description: Advocated a dualism separating mind and matter, body and spirit, connecting the scientific view of nature with the metaphysics of the mind.

keyAchievements.title: Discovered the Law of Refraction of Light
keyAchievements.description: Through optical research, he independently proved the law of refraction of light, also known as 'Snell's Law'.

faq.question: Why is Descartes called the 'father of modern philosophy'?
faq.answer: He is referred to as the founder of modern philosophy by first establishing the basic framework of modern thought, and he opened the path for modern mathematics by founding analytic geometry.

faq.question: What is Descartes' most famous proposition?
faq.answer: In his major work 'Discourse on the Method', 'I think, therefore I am (Cogito, ergo sum)' is famous for first establishing the fundamental principle of the Enlightenment's 'autonomous and rational subject'.

faq.question: What were Descartes' religious beliefs?
faq.answer: He maintained his Roman Catholic faith throughout his life and claimed that one of the purposes of 'Meditations on First Philosophy' was to defend the Christian faith.

faq.question: What is Descartes' dualism?
faq.answer: He divided substance into two types: mental ('thinking thing') and material ('extended thing'), arguing that mind and body are two distinctly separate substances.

timeline.event: Born in the Republic of Geneva, Switzerland, to Isaac Rousseau, a watchmaker, and Suzanne Bernard.
His mother, Suzanne Bernard, died from complications of childbirth.
His father moved due to a dispute with the city of Geneva, and Rousseau entered a boarding school near Geneva.
Began receiving vocational training to become a court clerk.
Received Roman Catholic baptism in Turin, Italy, and was given the baptismal name Jean-Joseph.
Moved to Lausanne and lived as a music teacher under a pseudonym.
Visited Paris for the first time, then lived in places like Chambéry via Lyon.
Lived near Madame de Warens in Chambéry and Les Charmettes, devoting himself to music and reading to build his education.
Met and formed friendships with the Enlightenment figures d'Alembert and Diderot in Paris.
Organized a new system of musical notation and presented it at the French Academy, but it was not successful.
After 'Dissertation sur la musique moderne' was published, he moved to Venice to serve as secretary to the Comte de Montaigu.
Married the maid Marie-Thérèse Levasseur after a long period of cohabitation.
Made a name for himself when his 'Discourse on the Arts and Sciences' won first prize in an academic essay contest held by the Academy of Dijon, and his opera 'Le Devin du village' was performed.
Participated in the compilation of Diderot and d'Alembert's 'Encyclopédie', writing articles on music and political economy, and published the 'Discourse on the Arts and Sciences'.
Composed the opera 'Le Devin du village'.
Immersed himself in political and social institutions for an essay contest by the Academy of Dijon, forming the basis for 'The Social Contract'.
Settled at the Hermitage and began writing 'The Social Contract', 'Julie, or the New Heloise', and 'Emile'.
Had a disagreement with Diderot over the social function of theater and broke off their friendship a year later.
'Julie, or the New Heloise' was published in Paris and achieved great success.
Published 'Emile' and 'The Social Contract', which led to persecution and his fleeing to Switzerland.
Began writing 'Confessions' after being attacked by Voltaire's anonymous work 'The Sentiment of the Citizens'.
Completed 'Confessions'.
Returned to Paris.
Wrote 'Rousseau Judge of Jean-Jacques'.
Began writing 'Reveries of the Solitary Walker' but failed to finish it.
Died on July 2.

keyAchievements.title: The Social Contract
keyAchievements.description: A book that perfected the theory of popular sovereignty and established the general will as the condition justifying the exercise of power.

keyAchievements.title: Discourse on the Arts and Sciences
keyAchievements.description: The first-prize winning essay at the Academy of Dijon, containing ideas that go beyond the limits of the Enlightenment.

keyAchievements.title: Discourse on the Origin of Inequality
keyAchievements.description: A work that explains the inequality arising from the development of property systems and social organizations by contrasting it with the state of nature.

keyAchievements.title: Emile
keyAchievements.description: Described a theory of natural religion that greatly influenced 18th-century intellectual circles, though it faced persecution.

keyAchievements.title: Participation in the Compilation of the Encyclopédie
keyAchievements.description: Participated in the compilation of Diderot and d'Alembert's 'Encyclopédie', writing articles on music and political economy.

keyAchievements.title: Theory of the General Will
keyAchievements.description: Proposed the concept of human nature's will for a form of association seeking the common good and equal liberty.

keyAchievements.title: Radical Egalitarian Ideas
keyAchievements.description: Delved into the issue of human equality practically for the first time in history, and asserted that economic inequality is the root of all social evils.

faq.question: Who was Jean-Jacques Rousseau?
faq.answer: Jean-Jacques Rousseau was a French social contract theorist, direct democrat, republican, and Enlightenment philosopher born in the Republic of Geneva, Switzerland.

faq.question: What does Rousseau's 'general will' mean?
faq.answer: Rousseau's general will is the will to seek a form of association that defends and protects the person and goods of each associate with the whole common force, and in which each, while uniting himself with all, may still obey himself alone, and remain as free as before.

faq.question: Why did Rousseau argue to 'return to nature'?
faq.answer: Rousseau did not reject civilization itself, but rather criticized the absurdities and contradictions of a civilized society that lacked freedom and equality, and strongly advocated restoring the archetype of a society that guarantees human's natural rights of freedom and equality.

faq.question: What are Rousseau's major works?
faq.answer: Rousseau's major works include 'The Social Contract', 'Discourse on the Arts and Sciences', 'Discourse on the Origin of Inequality', 'Emile', and 'Julie, or the New Heloise'.

timeline.event: Born in Freiberg in Mähren, Moravia, Austrian Empire.
Acknowledged the lack of results in his research on eel reproductive organs in his first paper 'On the Testes of the Eel'.
Received his medical degree with the thesis 'On the Spinal Cord of Lower Fish Species'.
Stayed in Paris for collaborative research with Jean-Martin Charcot.
Married Martha Bernays and opened his own neurology clinic.
Published 'Studies on Hysteria' co-authored with Josef Breuer.
Published 'The Interpretation of Dreams'.
First detected abnormalities in his oral cavity.
A tumor was first discovered in his oral cavity.
Received the Goethe Prize in recognition of his contributions to psychology and German literature.
The Nazis took power in Germany, and many of his books were burned.
Fled to London, England after Nazi Germany's annexation of Austria.
Died.

keyAchievements.title: Founded Psychoanalysis
keyAchievements.description: As an Austrian psychologist, he founded the discipline of psychoanalysis.

keyAchievements.title: Theory of the Unconscious and Repression
keyAchievements.description: Invented theories on the unconscious and the defense mechanism of repression.

keyAchievements.title: Psychoanalytic Clinical Treatment
keyAchievements.description: Invented clinical treatment methods for treating mental pathologies through dialogue between the patient and the psychoanalyst.

keyAchievements.title: Redefinition of Sexuality
keyAchievements.description: Redefined sexuality as a primary motivating energy in human life.

keyAchievements.title: Theory of Transference
keyAchievements.description: Invented the theory of emotional transference in the therapeutic relationship.

keyAchievements.title: Dream Interpretation Techniques
keyAchievements.description: Invented techniques for observing 'unconscious desires' through dreams.

keyAchievements.title: Concept of the Mental Structure
keyAchievements.description: Stated that the mind consists of the conscious, preconscious, and unconscious, and proposed the concepts of the id, ego, and superego.

keyAchievements.title: Instinct Theory
keyAchievements.description: Proposed the instinct towards life (Libido) and the drive towards death (Thanatos).

faq.question: Who is Sigmund Freud?
faq.answer: Sigmund Freud was an Austrian psychologist and the founder of psychoanalysis.

faq.question: What are the main theories created by Freud?
faq.answer: He created theories on the unconscious and the defense mechanism of repression, psychoanalytic clinical treatment methods, and the theory defining sexuality as the main motivating energy.

faq.question: How did Freud explain the structure of the mind?
faq.answer: Freud stated that the mind consists of the conscious, preconscious, and unconscious, and he proposed the concepts of the id, ego, and superego.

faq.question: When did Freud die?
faq.answer: Sigmund Freud died on September 23, 1939.

timeline.event: Born in Switzerland.
His father, Paul Jung, was appointed to a larger parish in Laufen (when Jung was 6).
Met Sigmund Freud for the first time in Vienna.
Began developing his analytical psychology theories independently.
Resigned from the International Psychoanalytical Association.
Served as president of the International Psychoanalytical Association.
Published the book 'Psychology of the Unconscious'.
Published the book 'A Psychological Approach to the Dogma of the Trinity'.
Published the book 'Transformation Symbolism in the Mass'.
Published the book 'Answer to Job'.
Passed away at the age of 85.
The book 'Memories, Dreams, Reflections' was published (posthumous publication).

keyAchievements.title: Founded Analytical Psychology
keyAchievements.description: As a Swiss psychiatrist, he pursued an independent path and pioneered and founded the field of analytical psychology.

keyAchievements.title: Established the Concept of Complex
keyAchievements.description: Used the word 'complex' to describe specific states and laid the foundation for the related theories.

keyAchievements.title: Established the Concept of the Collective Unconscious
keyAchievements.description: Established the concept of the collective unconscious, a repository of mental materials that are part of the personal unconscious but shared through the history and culture of humanity rather than personal experience.

keyAchievements.title: Personality Classification (Introversion/Extraversion)
keyAchievements.description: Classified personalities into introverted and extraverted types.

keyAchievements.title: Developed the Concept of Individuation
keyAchievements.description: Developed and theorized psychotherapy, viewing that individuals undergoing psychotherapy could advance into whole persons through the process of 'individuation'.

keyAchievements.title: Asserted the Self Archetype
keyAchievements.description: Asserted that the self archetype, which allows the unconscious within humans to realize a unified whole, possesses a transcendent function.

keyAchievements.title: Concepts of Anima and Animus
keyAchievements.description: Presented the concepts of anima and animus, which represent the opposite gender in a person's psyche.

keyAchievements.title: Concept of Archetype
keyAchievements.description: Established the concept of the archetype, a term borrowed from anthropology meaning universal human mental images or themes.

keyAchievements.title: Concept of Persona
keyAchievements.description: Presented the concept of persona, an aspect of personality that appears for adaptation or personal convenience.

keyAchievements.title: Concept of the Shadow
keyAchievements.description: Presented the concept of the shadow, which includes repressed and poorly known aspects of the personality often considered negative.

keyAchievements.title: Concept of the Self
keyAchievements.description: Viewed the 'self', a central concept governing the individuation process and symbolized by a mandala that unifies the feminine and masculine, as the main archetype of the psyche.

faq.question: Who is Carl Jung?
faq.answer: Carl Jung was a Swiss psychiatrist, the founder of analytical psychology, who established the concepts of the complex and collective unconscious, and classified personalities into introverted and extraverted types.

faq.question: Why did Carl Jung and Freud part ways?
faq.answer: Freud restricted the concept of 'libido' to sexuality, while Jung saw it more broadly as general energy, which led to a divergence of views and their split.

faq.question: What is the field of psychology founded by Carl Jung?
faq.answer: Carl Jung pursued his own path and pioneered and founded the field of analytical psychology.

faq.question: How did Carl Jung speak about God?
faq.answer: He mentioned about God in a broadcast interview, saying, "I do not believe in Him, I know Him."

timeline.event: Born to a family of Portuguese-Jewish descent in Amsterdam, Netherlands.
When his father died, he took over the import goods shop run by his father at the age of 21.
Was excommunicated and cursed from the Jewish community on the pretext of criticizing Judaism and blaspheming God.
Wrote 'Treatise on the Emendation of the Intellect' in Rijnsburg and published 'Principles of Cartesian Philosophy'.
Formed a relationship with Johan de Witt, a political leader of the Dutch Republic, in Voorburg.
Published 'Tractatus Theologico-Politicus' anonymously and settled in The Hague that year.
'Tractatus Theologico-Politicus' was banned from publication by the Dutch government.
Began writing 'Tractatus Politicus'.
Passed away from tuberculosis at the age of 44.
Completed his lifelong work 'Ethics' but could not publish it during his lifetime; the first edition of 'Tractatus Politicus' was published.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote the 'Ethics'
keyAchievements.description: Partially accepting Descartes' foundationalism while adopting a philosophical stance against mind-body dualism in his masterpiece 'Ethics', which significantly influenced Western philosophy.

keyAchievements.title: Unified Natural Substance and Moral Essence
keyAchievements.description: Revived the ideas of the ancient Stoic school in a modern sense, undertaking the philosophical task of unifying natural substance and moral essence.

keyAchievements.title: Presented the Concept of Conatus
keyAchievements.description: Presented the concept of 'Conatus', the desire or endeavor to preserve, develop, and perfect the self, and saw its full expression as happiness.

keyAchievements.title: Established Monistic Pantheism
keyAchievements.description: Viewed God and Nature as two names for the same substance, establishing a monistic pantheistic worldview with strong atheistic tendencies.

keyAchievements.title: Contributions to Modern Rationalist Philosophy
keyAchievements.description: Recognized as one of the major rationalist philosophers of 17th-century Europe who laid the foundation for the 18th-century Enlightenment and modern biblical criticism.

faq.question: How was Spinoza treated in the Jewish community?
faq.answer: Although well-versed in Jewish culture, he was expelled from the Jewish community at age 23 due to his critical ideas, and all his works were placed on the Catholic Church's Index of Forbidden Books.

faq.question: What are Spinoza's main philosophical ideas?
faq.answer: He argued for monistic pantheism, viewing God and nature as two names for the same substance, and presented the concept of 'Conatus', the desire to preserve, develop, and perfect the self.

faq.question: How did Spinoza make a living?
faq.answer: He barely made a living by grinding optical lenses and rejected any compensation or honors, including professorships, during his lifetime.

faq.question: What are Spinoza's representative works?
faq.answer: His lifelong masterpiece is 'Ethics, Demonstrated in Geometrical Order' ('Ethics'), and others include 'Tractatus Theologico-Politicus' and 'Treatise on the Emendation of the Intellect'.

faq.question: Is the quote 'Even if the world were to end tomorrow, I would plant an apple tree today' by Spinoza?
faq.answer: These words were spoken by Martin Luther, and it is explicitly stated in the original text that it is unclear whether Spinoza actually said it.

timeline.event: Birth of Sun Tzu (Estimated)
Became a military strategist for King Helü of Wu upon the recommendation of Wu Zixu
Launched an expedition against Chu with Helü and Wu Zixu as commanders, capturing the Chu capital, Ying
Despite Sun Tzu's opposition, Helü attacked Yue, was defeated, and died
Death of Sun Tzu (Estimated)

keyAchievements.title: Authored The Art of War
keyAchievements.description: As a strategist of the Spring and Autumn period, he authored the 13 chapters of 'The Art of War'.

keyAchievements.title: Appointed Military Strategist of Wu
keyAchievements.description: Became the military strategist for King Helü of Wu upon the recommendation of Wu Zixu and trained the army.

keyAchievements.title: Victorious Expedition against Chu
keyAchievements.description: In 506 BC, contributed to the capture of the capital Ying by commanding the expedition against Chu with Helü.

keyAchievements.title: Assisted in the Great Victory over Yue
keyAchievements.description: Assisted Helü's successor, Fuchai, in building national strength and then attacking Yue, contributing to a great victory.

faq.question: Who is Sun Tzu?
faq.answer: Sun Tzu was a strategist of the Spring and Autumn period, whose courtesy name was Changqing, better known by the honorific Sun Tzu. He wrote 'The Art of War'.

faq.question: Are the records of Sun Tzu's life clear?
faq.answer: There are important records like Sima Qian's Records of the Grand Historian, but his life is a subject of debate, and his life after retirement is unknown.

faq.question: How did Sun Tzu become a military strategist for Wu?
faq.answer: He fled to Wu with his clan, got to know the chancellor Wu Zixu, and was invited by King Helü of Wu to become a military strategist upon Wu Zixu's recommendation.

faq.question: What is Sun Tzu's representative work?
faq.answer: Sun Tzu's representative work is 'The Art of War', which was treated as an important military text in the Sinosphere for a long time.

timeline.event: Born in Chirnside, north of Edinburgh.
Changed his surname from Home to Hume, and worked as a clerk in Bristol for a few months.
At age 26, completed his first philosophical work, 'A Treatise of Human Nature'.
Applied for a professorship at the University of Edinburgh with essays on morals and politics but failed.
Served as a tutor to the Marquess of Annandale, and began writing 'The History of England'.
Published 'An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding'.
Published 'An Enquiry Concerning the Principles of Morals'.
Returned to Edinburgh and served as the Keeper of the Advocates' Library.
Published the 6-volume 'The History of England', written over 15 years.
Published 'Four Dissertations' and 'The Natural History of Religion'.
Published 'Sister Peg'.
Worked as secretary to the British Ambassador to France and formed a friendship with Rousseau.
Published 'My Own Life', died on August 25, and was buried in Edinburgh.
'Dialogues Concerning Natural Religion' was published posthumously.

keyAchievements.title: Role as a Philosopher, Economist, and Historian
keyAchievements.description: A Scottish philosopher, economist, and historian who is one of the leading figures associated with Western philosophy and the Scottish Enlightenment.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote 'A Treatise of Human Nature'
keyAchievements.description: At age 26, he wrote his first philosophical work, 'A Treatise of Human Nature', which is considered one of the most important books in the history of philosophy today.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote 'The History of England'
keyAchievements.description: Wrote 'The History of England' over 15 years, publishing it in 6 volumes from 1754 to 1762.

keyAchievements.title: Raised the 'Is-Ought Problem'
keyAchievements.description: Raised the 'is-ought problem', stating that merely describing 'facts' cannot lead to normative conclusions about what 'ought' to be, establishing it as a core issue in modern metaethics.

keyAchievements.title: Presented 'Sympathy' as the Psychological Basis of Morality
keyAchievements.description: Viewed morality as a matter of feeling and established sympathy as the psychological foundation of morality, becoming a precursor to utilitarian moral theory.

keyAchievements.title: Advocated an Empirical Methodology for Human Sciences
keyAchievements.description: Advocated for human science as a science studied by the empirical method of 'experience and observation', attempting to analyze the basic facts of the human mind.

keyAchievements.title: Raised the Problem of Induction
keyAchievements.description: Raised the 'problem of induction', arguing that the reliability of inductive reasoning is not guaranteed.

keyAchievements.title: Skeptical Stance on Causality
keyAchievements.description: Doubted the problem of causality, viewing that causality is actually just a probability established inductively through habit.

keyAchievements.title: Clarified the Role of Goods in the Market Economy
keyAchievements.description: Argued that money (goods) is a medium of exchange in the market system, and that the amount of money holds no meaning in a society that has reached a state of equilibrium.

faq.question: Who was David Hume?
faq.answer: David Hume was a Scottish philosopher, economist, and historian, and one of the leading figures associated with Western philosophy and the Scottish Enlightenment.

faq.question: What are the characteristics of Hume's philosophy?
faq.answer: Hume's philosophy is generally viewed as an extension of skepticism, and there is debate over whether naturalistic elements are treated as important.

faq.question: What was Hume's stance on religion?
faq.answer: Hume expressed his views on all religions, but because talking about religion was delicate at the time, his works concerning religion were published posthumously.

faq.question: What were Hume's political views?
faq.answer: Hume belonged to the skeptical and moderate Tory party, and emphasized the importance of the order of law and the public spirit for the community.

faq.question: How did Hume view the economy?
faq.answer: Hume believed that commerce and trade would raise people's living standards and bring cultural refinement, and that the dictatorial power of a government would weaken if the nation became affluent.

timeline.event: Born as the son of a country lawyer in Wrington, Somerset.
Entered Westminster School and graduated with excellent grades.
Entered Christ Church, Oxford, as a scholar and encountered Descartes' philosophy.
Received his Bachelor of Arts.
Began working as a tutor at Oxford University for 5 years.
Began studying natural sciences and medicine.
While staying in Brandenburg, Germany as an embassy secretary, witnessed the possibility of religious tolerance.
Appointed secretary to the Board of Trade and Plantations.
Began writing his major work, 'An Essay Concerning Human Understanding'.
Returned to England.
Fled to the Netherlands to escape the Royalists.
Returned to England following the accession of William III through the Glorious Revolution.
Became Commissioner of Appeals and became famous by publishing 'An Essay Concerning Human Understanding', which he wrote while in exile.
Retired to Essex.
Died in Essex.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of British Empiricism
keyAchievements.description: Evaluated as the first full-fledged British empiricist philosopher.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed the Social Contract Theory
keyAchievements.description: Argued that the state emerged through a social contract to ensure the security of natural rights.

keyAchievements.title: Enlightenment and Liberal Thinker
keyAchievements.description: Widely known as one of the most influential Enlightenment thinkers and liberal theorists.

keyAchievements.title: Origin of the Concept of 'Self-Identity'
keyAchievements.description: The first philosopher to define the self through the continuity of 'consciousness'.

keyAchievements.title: Advocated the 'Blank Slate' (Tabula Rasa)
keyAchievements.description: Argued that people are born without innate ideas, viewing the mind as a 'blank slate'.

keyAchievements.title: Proposed the Night-Watchman State Theory
keyAchievements.description: Argued for a night-watchman state, stating that the state's duty lies in ensuring a minimum level of security.

keyAchievements.title: Laid the Foundation for Popular Sovereignty
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundation for popular sovereignty by arguing that the people entrust power to the state through a contract.

keyAchievements.title: Advocated Constitutional Democracy and Natural Rights
keyAchievements.description: The first person to advocate for constitutional democracy and natural rights.

keyAchievements.title: Laid the Foundation for the Separation of Powers
keyAchievements.description: Laid the foundation for the separation of powers among the legislative, judicial, and executive branches.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote 'An Essay Concerning Human Understanding'
keyAchievements.description: His major work dealing with the problem of cognition, including modern science.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote 'The Reasonableness of Christianity'
keyAchievements.description: A text written as a response to John Toland's draft.

keyAchievements.title: Wrote 'A Letter Concerning Toleration'
keyAchievements.description: Argued that 'toleration is the most certain mark of the true church'.

faq.question: What kind of philosopher is John Locke considered to be?
faq.answer: Locke is evaluated as the first full-fledged British empiricist philosopher, and is known as one of the most influential Enlightenment thinkers and liberal theorists.

faq.question: What are John Locke's major works?
faq.answer: His major works include 'An Essay Concerning Human Understanding', 'Two Treatises of Government', 'The Reasonableness of Christianity', and 'A Letter Concerning Toleration'.

faq.question: Who did John Locke's ideas influence?
faq.answer: Locke's ideas influenced Voltaire, Rousseau, the American Revolution, and Scottish Enlightenment thinkers, and are also reflected in the United States Declaration of Independence.

faq.question: What is the 'blank slate' advocated by John Locke?
faq.answer: Locke viewed the mind as a 'blank slate' (tabula rasa), arguing that people are born without innate ideas.

faq.question: What does John Locke's social contract theory argue?
faq.answer: Locke argued that the state emerged through a social contract to ensure the safety of natural rights, including holding one's own property through labor in a peaceful state of nature.

faq.question: What was John Locke's stance on toleration?
faq.answer: Locke argued that 'toleration is the most certain mark of the true church', but he showed a contradictory attitude by excluding Roman Catholics or atheists from being objects of toleration.

faq.question: What was John Locke's stance on slavery?
faq.answer: Locke showed a contradictory attitude by opposing slavery in England while accepting the ownership of black slaves by English people in the American colonies.
